metadata.summary: Value-first email marketing: list building, welcome sequences, segmentation, sales sequences. Never purchase lists—spam complaints above 0.1% trigger sanctions. Build organically with lead magnets.

content.title: Email Marketing
content.summary: A value-first email marketing framework covering list building, welcome sequences, newsletter strategy, segmentation, and sales email sequences. Never purchase or rent lists — spam complaints above 0.1% trigger Google/Yahoo bulk sender sanctions. Build organically with lead magnets; automate the welcome sequence before any broadcast.

why.p: Email is the only audience channel you fully own — social algorithms can eliminate your reach overnight. But most email marketing fails because it prioritizes promotion over value (80% value, 20% pitch is the standard), sends irregularly, or skips the welcome sequence that captures subscribers at peak engagement. A structured Build→Nurture→Segment→Convert→Retain flow consistently produces 20-30%+ open rates where ad-driven lists produce single digits.

list.item: Launching a new product and building an email list from scratch
Setting up automated welcome and nurture sequences for an existing audience
Converting social media followers to an owned channel
Running a promotional campaign (product launch, sale, cohort open)
Re-engaging an inactive or cold subscriber list
Audience is still undefined — list-building without ICP clarity creates churn
Fewer than 50 subscribers with no consistent content cadence — focus on content first
Pure B2B enterprise with procurement-driven sales — email alone won't close
Compliance requirements prevent automated outreach (certain financial or healthcare contexts)

section.name: list-building
section.p: Email list growth is the foundation of the strategy. Without organic list building rules in place, all subsequent tactics fail.

rule.statement: Never purchase or rent email lists; spam complaints above 0.1% trigger Google/Yahoo bulk sender sanctions that are difficult to reverse.
rule.rationale: Purchased lists have high complaint rates because recipients did not opt in. ISP blacklisting from complaint spikes can render the sending domain unusable for months.

rule.statement: Provide a lead magnet before asking for an email address; organic list growth without a lead magnet is near-zero on most platforms.
rule.rationale: The default "subscribe to my newsletter" CTA converts at 0.1-0.5%; a specific lead magnet converts at 3-10%. The lead magnet is the primary driver of list growth.

list.item: Checklist: high conversion, easy consumption (e.g., "10-Point SEO Checklist")
Template: high conversion, immediate value (e.g., "Email Swipe File")
Guide/eBook: medium conversion, deep knowledge (e.g., "Complete Guide to X")
Mini-course: medium conversion, nurtures relationship (e.g., "5-Day Email Course")
Tool/Calculator: high conversion, interactive utility (e.g., "ROI Calculator")
Webinar: high-intent, live event (converts best when topic is urgent)

rule.statement: Use a single email field (no name, no phone) for maximum conversion; each additional field reduces opt-ins by 20-30%.
rule.rationale: Friction on the sign-up form is the primary cause of low list growth rates; removing the name field alone typically lifts conversion 15-25%.

list.item: Above-the-fold placement converts at 2-3x sidebar placement
Clear value proposition in the headline: what they get, not "subscribe"
Privacy assurance below the form ("No spam. Unsubscribe anytime.")
Exit-intent popup: use sparingly, only with high-value lead magnet

section.name: welcome-sequence

rule.statement: Automate a 5-email welcome sequence triggered immediately on signup; the first 7 days after opt-in is the highest engagement window.
rule.rationale: New subscribers have peak curiosity on sign-up day. Brands that send nothing for the first week lose 40-60% of potential engagement before the first broadcast.

list.item: Day 0: Deliver lead magnet + brief self-introduction
Day 1: Share your story and philosophy; build connection
Day 3: Best content piece (top blog post, resource, case study)
Day 5: Social proof or case study from a customer
Day 7: Soft pitch — mention the product, no hard sell

section.reference: 5-email welcome sequence with subject lines and body scaffolding

list.item: By engagement: Active (opened last 30 days), Engaged (last 90), Inactive (90+ days), VIP (high engagement + purchasers)
By lead source: different messaging per acquisition channel
By purchase history: upsell relevant products to buyers
By interests: targeted content per declared interest

section.name: newsletter-strategy

rule.statement: Follow the 80/20 rule: 80% value content, 20% promotional; deviating toward more promotion consistently increases unsubscribe rate.
rule.rationale: Subscribers who feel a list is primarily promotional unsubscribe at 3-5x the rate of subscribers who receive value-first content. Trust is the deliverable.

list.item: Curated: 5-7 links + commentary — suits thought leadership positioning
Educational: 1 deep topic per issue — best for teaching-based brands
Personal: story + lesson — highest reply rates, builds relationship
Mixed: main insight + bonus items — most common for product-adjacent newsletters

section.reference: Weekly newsletter format with hook, insight, quick wins, and CTA

section.name: sales-launch-sequence

rule.statement: Run the full 8-day launch sequence for product launches; compressing to 3-4 days consistently reduces conversion by 40-60%.
rule.rationale: The sequence allows different audience segments to convert at different points: early birds on day 0, consideration buyers on days 1-3, deadline buyers on days 5-6.

section.reference: 8-day product launch email sequence with timing and structure

list.item: Day -7: announcement + value preview
Day -3: deep dive on the problem
Day -1: early bird reminder
Day 0: launch + full details
Day 1: FAQ and objection handling
Day 3: case study or testimonial
Day 5: last chance and scarcity
Day 6: final reminder (hours left)
Open rate: 20-25% good, 30%+ great (note: iOS Mail Privacy inflates; use click rate as primary signal)
Click rate: 2-3% good, 5%+ great
Unsubscribe rate: below 0.5% acceptable, below 0.2% great
List growth rate: 5%/month good, 10%+ great

rule.statement: Set up SPF, DKIM, and DMARC on the sending domain before the first broadcast; fix deliverability infrastructure before you need it.
rule.rationale: Google and Yahoo 2024+ bulk sender requirements mandate DMARC; missing it causes emails to be rejected or junked from day one of any broadcast campaign.

rule.statement: Send from a personal name ("Alex from Acme") rather than a brand name for personal-tone sequences.
rule.rationale: Personal sender names consistently produce higher open rates across platforms because they do not trigger "promotional" mental filtering.

rule.statement: Re-engage or remove subscribers inactive for 180+ days before they degrade sender reputation.
rule.rationale: Inactive subscribers lower the engagement rate that ISPs use to evaluate sender quality; a suppression campaign before large broadcasts protects deliverability.

antipattern.description: Sending to a purchased or cold list.
antipattern.reason: High complaint rates trigger ISP blacklisting that can make the sending domain unusable for months.

antipattern.description: No welcome sequence — sending nothing in the first 7 days after opt-in.
antipattern.reason: The first week is the highest-engagement window; missing it loses 40-60% of potential activation.

antipattern.description: Inconsistent sending schedule — emailing only when there is something to promote.
antipattern.reason: Irregular emails erode trust and train subscribers to ignore them; a regular schedule builds expectation and open habit.

section.name: agent-integration-guidelines
section.p: Agents can draft full email sequences (welcome, nurture, re-engagement) given a brief with ICP, product description, and tone guidelines. Claude Sonnet handles email copywriting efficiently; Opus is appropriate for designing the full segmentation strategy or a multi-touch launch sequence. Agents should not send emails autonomously — every batch should pass a human review checkpoint before delivery.

rule.statement: Behavioral trigger logic (e.g., "send only if user has not logged in for 7 days") must be configured inside the ESP, not hallucinated by the agent.
rule.rationale: Agent-produced trigger descriptions may be logically ambiguous; ESP configuration UI forces clarity and prevents misconfiguration.

list.item: Merge tag validation varies by ESP (`{{first_name}}` vs `{first_name}`) — always specify the ESP format in the prompt
Segmentation logic must be verified in the ESP UI before automation activation; wrong conditions silently fire to wrong segment
Agents tend to write longer emails than needed — specify a word count constraint in every prompt
Subject lines with excessive punctuation or spam trigger words (FREE!!!, 100% guaranteed) will tank deliverability; always run through a checker before scheduling
A/B test copy variants should be staged in the ESP, not run simultaneously by multiple agent calls

template.purpose: 5-email welcome sequence with subject lines and body scaffolding
Weekly newsletter format with hook, insight, quick wins, and CTA
8-day product launch email sequence with timing and structure
